title: Right-to-erasure propagation completeness across downstream systems
exclusions: Does NOT validate erasure from third-party processors (see UC-22.44.1) or legitimately-retained records under Art.17(3) exceptions.
evidence: Saved search 'erasure_propagation_gap' runs hourly and writes system-by-system deletion evidence to compliance_summary; evidence-pack report per erasure request.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.17 right to erasure requires that the data be erased 'without undue delay'. The UC tracks completion across every downstream system listed in the ROPA.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.17 (Right to erasure) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.36.2: Right-to-erasure propagation completeness across downstream systems.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.36.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.17(2) requires reasonable measures to inform third parties; this UC surfaces the subset of third-party processors that acknowledged the erasure, but the DPO must verify the remainder manually.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.17(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.36.2: Right-to-erasure propagation completeness across downstream systems.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.36.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: §1798.105 consumer right to delete; the UC tracks deletion across every system registered in the business's consumer-facing record.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that CCPA/CPRA §1798.105 (Consumer right to delete) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.36.2: Right-to-erasure propagation completeness across downstream systems.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When an erasure ticket is closed but at least one registered downstream system has no deletion_ack within 7 days, the UC fires naming the subject_token and the system(s) missing the ack.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Systems covered by a lawful-retention exception (retention_hold=true in erasure_exceptions.csv) do NOT fire.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=dsar subject_token=* action=erasure earliest=-180d
| stats values(system) as acked_systems by subject_token ticket_id
| lookup ropa_system_map.csv ticket_id OUTPUT systems_in_scope
| eval missing=mvfilter(NOT match(acked_systems, system))
| where mvcount(missing)>0
| table ticket_id subject_token systems_in_scope acked_systems missing
description: Enforces that every registered downstream system reports a deletion acknowledgement for each accepted erasure request within seven days. A subject_token that persists past the SLA in any system is a direct Art.17 / CPRA §1798.105 finding.
value: Moves the erasure claim from 'we believe it propagated' to 'we proved it propagated, system by system' — exactly the evidence supervisory authorities ask for on first inspection.
implementation: (1) Publish a ropa_system_map.csv lookup keyed by ticket_id → list of systems-in-scope; (2) ingest deletion-ack events from each system (DB TTL jobs, DWH deletion manifests, app-level hooks); (3) run hourly; (4) integrate with SOAR to auto-reopen tickets with missing acks. Indexes required: `index=dsar`.
visualization: Matrix of ticket_id × system with green/red cells; aging histogram; single-value for 'in-scope systems with ack in SLA'.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `dsar` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `ropa_system_map.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('ropa_system_map.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.17`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.17 (Right to erasure) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.36.2: Right-to-erasure propagation completeness across downstream systems." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `dsar` under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Publish a ropa_system_map.csv lookup keyed by ticket_id → list of systems-in-scope. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** ingest deletion-ack events from each system (DB TTL jobs, DWH deletion manifests, app-level hooks). Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 3:** run hourly. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** integrate with SOAR to auto-reopen tickets with missing acks. Automated remediation must be gated behind a defined approval workflow; compliance controls that take destructive action without human review are themselves a governance risk.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Right-to-erasure propagation completeness across downstream systems," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the GDPR control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `ropa_system_map.csv` — the enrichment table that adds compliance-relevant business context to raw events. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`systems_in_scope`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `ticket_id`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `ropa_system_map.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval ticket_id = lower(ticket_id)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 180 days.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.36.2: Right-to-erasure propagation completeness across downstream ]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -180d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.36.2,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=dsar sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `acked_systems`, `missing`, `subject_token`, `systems_in_scope`, `ticket_id`. Run:

```spl
index=dsar sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table acked_systems, missing, subject_token, systems_in_scope, ticket_id
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `ropa_system_map.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup ropa_system_map.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.36.2: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Matrix Of Ticket Id × System With Green/Red Cells; Aging Histogram; Single-Value For 'In-Scope Systems With Ack In Sla' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see when data-subject requests are running late so privacy duties stay on track across systems.
